claims.text: In 1972 the CIA launched a funded program to research ESP, mind reading, and telekinesis for espionage purposes, in response to reports of Soviet psychic research.

evidence.quote:
in 1972 a classified report was
circulating in the american intelligence
community the report claimed that the
soviet union was pouring millions into
programs to research esp mind reading
and telekinesis

claims.rationale: The 1972 origin of CIA/DIA-funded psychic research programs (initially at SRI) is documented in the declassified Stargate Collection released by the CIA in 2017. The Soviet-threat framing is also standard in the historical record.

claims.text: The CIA's remote-viewing programs were consolidated into Project Stargate at Fort Meade, Maryland, and ran for about 23 years before being shut down in 1995 after Nightline reported on it.

evidence.quote:
this became known as project stargate
and project stargate ran for years it
was finally shut down in 1995 after
nightline reported about it

claims.rationale: Fort Meade hosted the program after SRI's early-1970s Stanford work; Stargate was publicly disclosed in 1995 and terminated that same year. This is well-documented.

dependencies.rationale: Names the successor institutional form of the 1972 program.

claims.text: In 1976 CIA psychic researcher Rosemary Smith at Patterson Air Force Base in Ohio allegedly located a downed Russian bomber in the Congo via remote viewing, and a paramilitary team found the plane at her coordinates.

evidence.quote:
in 1976 a
russian bomber went down in the congo
and the details of the crash were sent
to cia psychic researchers operating out
of patterson air base in ohio

claims.rationale: The Congo-bomber anecdote (Tu-22 recovery attributed to Rosemary Smith and reportedly praised by Jimmy Carter) is widely repeated in Stargate lore but historians note the recovery is underdocumented. Omitting directTruth since it rests on a single widely-repeated story.

dependencies.rationale: Concrete early success story of the CIA program.

claims.text: On May 22, 1984 Joe McMoneagle used remote viewing to describe ancient Mars from supplied coordinates, reporting tall thin beings, pyramids, and obelisk-like structures; the envelope revealed 'Mars, approximately 1 million years BC' afterward.

evidence.quote:
his strangest mission took place on
may 22nd 1984. here's how it went
mcmonagle was in a room with one other
person a researcher he was given a
sealed envelope

the envelope is torn open and on it
reads the planet mars time of interest
approximately 1 million years bc

claims.rationale: The 1984 Mars viewing session exists as an actual declassified Stargate document. However, the content of McMoneagle's 'vision' is experiential data, not verified fact about Mars. Omitting directTruth because the host is reporting what the session produced, not endorsing its cosmological accuracy.

dependencies.rationale: A specific session from within Project Stargate.

claims.text: Joe McMoneagle was involved in 450 separate CIA missions as a remote-viewing psychic spy between 1978 and 1984 and was awarded the Legion of Merit on retirement from the Army.

evidence.quote:
between 1978 and 1984 mcmonicle was
involved in 450 separate cia missions

when he retired from the army
he was awarded the legion of merit for
outstanding service

claims.rationale: McMoneagle's military service, Stargate tenure, and Legion of Merit award are matters of public record. The 450 missions figure is his own tally and is widely cited.

claims.text: In 1989 remote viewer Angela Ford located a fugitive former customs agent to 'Lowell, Wyoming' — actually Lovell, Wyoming — where he was subsequently found.

evidence.quote:
in 1989 a remote
viewer named angela ford was asked to
help track a former customs agent who
was on the run and she told researchers
she was able to see him in lowell
wyoming

claims.rationale: The Angela Ford / Charles Jordan case (Lovell misheard/transcribed as Lowell) is a widely repeated Stargate success story, originally surfaced in remote-viewing literature. Lovell, Wyoming is a real town; Jordan was reportedly apprehended there. Omitting directTruth because independent corroboration outside RV advocacy sources is limited.

claims.text: According to the CIA, McMoneagle's remote viewing was successful about 20% of the time.

evidence.quote:
but according to the cia mcmonicle was
successful 20 of the time

claims.rationale: The '20% hit-rate' figure appears in Stargate-era CIA summaries but exactly what counted as a hit is contested; the official 1995 AIR review concluded RV was not useful for intelligence. Omitting directTruth because the number's meaning depends heavily on the coding protocol.

dependencies.rationale: Quantifies the success attributed to McMoneagle.

claims.text: US government research into ESP did not end with Stargate: in 2014 the Office of Naval Research launched a four-year $4 million program to study premonition and 'sixth sense' in sailors and marines.

evidence.quote:
in 2014 the
office of naval research launched a
four-year 4 million program to explore
the use of premonition or sixth sense
among sailors and marines

claims.rationale: The ONR 'Enhanced Human Performance' premonition program (c. 2014, ~$3.85M) is well documented in press reporting (e.g., Time, Guardian) and in ONR basic-research contracts.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] Undercuts the 'it was shut down in 1995 and that was the end' framing: equivalent research continued under new names.

claims.text: The CIA's Operation Acoustic Kitty — implanting microphones and transmitters in a live cat — cost roughly $20 million and failed when the cat was hit by a taxi on its first deployment.

evidence.quote:
acoustic kitty involved implanting a
microphone into a cat's ear canal and a
transmitter at the base of its skull

it
was hit by a car as soon as it was let
out of its cage ugh what did that little
experiment cost 20 million dollars

claims.rationale: Acoustic Kitty is confirmed in declassified CIA documents released via Victor Marchetti; the taxi-collision failure is the widely-reported anecdote although some historians dispute the exact cause of termination. Cost figure is approximate.

claims.text: The CIA document dump on Project Stargate and related psychic projects runs to roughly 12 million pages.

evidence.quote:
these
document dumps are always a little light
how many pages are we talking 25 50. 12
million

claims.rationale: The 2017 CREST release of CIA records was ~12 million pages total covering many programs, not all Stargate. Host's framing conflates the whole CREST dump with Stargate, so directTruth is moderated.

claims.text: ESP remains a research subject worthy of modest study despite its fringe reputation, given the far more trivial things Congress routinely funds.

evidence.quote:
if we can spend a million
dollars studying the effect of cocaine
on the sexual habits of quail
we can spend a few bucks on esp

claims.rationale: Host explicitly takes an 'open-minded skeptic' position. This is editorial advocacy, not an empirical claim — omitting directTruth.

claims.text: The framing that the US government has 'already been to Mars' via psychics is rhetorical, not a claim the host endorses as literally true.

evidence.quote:
what if i told you the cia
and united states military have already
been to mars but they didn't use
spaceships to get there
they used psychics

claims.rationale: Host opens with a hook framing, but treats McMoneagle's Mars session as historical Stargate artifact rather than confirmed Martian exploration. The self-labeled 'skeptical believer' stance and the refusal to assert the vision was veridical indicate low directTruth for the literal interpretation.
